# Aristotle

Greek philosopher

Ask Anything Quick Summary

Table of Contents

Table of Contents Quick Summary Ask Anything

Top Questions

### What did Aristotle do?

Aristotle was one of the greatest philosophers who ever lived and the first genuine scientist in history. He made pioneering contributions to all fields of philosophy and science, he invented the field of [formal logic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/formal-logic) , and he identified the various scientific disciplines and explored their relationships to each other. Aristotle was also a teacher and founded his own school in Athens, known as the [Lyceum](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Lyceum-Greek-philosophical-school) .

### Where did Aristotle live?

After his father died about 367 BCE, Aristotle journeyed to Athens, where he joined the Academy of Plato. He left the Academy upon Plato’s death about 348, traveling to the northwestern coast of present-day [Turkey](https://www.britannica.com/place/Turkey) . He lived there and on the island of [Lésbos](https://www.britannica.com/place/Lesbos-island-Greece) until 343 or 342, when [King Philip II](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Philip-II-king-of-Macedonia) of Macedonia summoned him to the Macedonian capital, [Pella](https://www.britannica.com/place/Pella-ancient-city-Greece) , to act as tutor to Philip’s young teenage son, Alexander, which he did for two or three years. Aristotle presumably lived somewhere in Macedonia until his (second) arrival in Athens in 335. In 323 hostility toward Macedonians in Athens prompted Aristotle to flee to the island of Euboea, where he died the following year.

### Who were Aristotle’s teachers and students?

Aristotle’s most famous teacher was [Plato](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Plato) (c. 428–c. 348 BCE), who himself had been a student of [Socrates](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Socrates) (c. 470–399 BCE). Socrates, [Plato](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Plato) , and Aristotle, whose lifetimes spanned a period of only about 150 years, remain among the most important figures in the history of Western philosophy. Aristotle’s most famous student was Philip II’s son Alexander, later to be known as [Alexander the Great](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Alexander-the-Great) , a military genius who eventually conquered the entire Greek world as well as [North Africa](https://www.britannica.com/place/North-Africa) and the [Middle East](https://www.britannica.com/place/Middle-East) . Aristotle’s most important philosophical student was probably [Theophrastus](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Theophrastus) , who became head of the Lyceum about 323.

### How many works did Aristotle write?

Aristotle wrote as many as 200 treatises and other works covering all areas of [philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy) and [science](https://www.britannica.com/science/science) . Of those, none survives in finished form. The approximately 30 works through which his thought was conveyed to later centuries consist of lecture notes (by Aristotle or his students) and draft manuscripts edited by ancient scholars, notably [Andronicus of Rhodes](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Andronicus-of-Rhodes) , the last head of the [Lyceum](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Lyceum-Greek-philosophical-school) , who arranged, edited, and published Aristotle’s extant works in Rome about 60 BCE. The naturally abbreviated style of these writings makes them difficult to read, even for philosophers.

### How did Aristotle influence subsequent philosophy and science?

Aristotle’s thought was original, profound, wide-ranging, and systematic. It eventually became the intellectual framework of Western [Scholasticism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Scholasticism) , the system of philosophical assumptions and problems characteristic of philosophy in western Europe during the [Middle Ages](https://www.britannica.com/event/Middle-Ages) . In the 13th century [St. Thomas Aquinas](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Thomas-Aquinas) undertook to reconcile Aristotelian philosophy and science with Christian dogma, and through him the theology and intellectual worldview of the Roman Catholic Church became Aristotelian. Since the mid-20th century, Aristotle’s ethics has inspired the field of virtue theory, an approach to ethics that emphasizes human well-being and the development of character. Aristotle’s thought also constitutes an important current in other fields of contemporary philosophy, especially metaphysics, political philosophy, and the philosophy of science.

**Aristotle** (born 384 bce , Stagira, [Chalcidice](https://www.britannica.com/place/Chalcidice-peninsula-Greece) , Greece—died 322, [Chalcis](https://www.britannica.com/place/Chalcis) , Euboea) was an ancient Greek philosopher and scientist, one of the greatest [intellectual](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/intellectual) figures of [Classical antiquity](https://www.britannica.com/event/Classical-antiquity) and Western history. He was the author of a [philosophical](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy) and scientific system that became the framework and vehicle for both [Christian](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Christianity) [Scholasticism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Scholasticism) and [medieval](https://www.britannica.com/event/Middle-Ages) [Islamic philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Islamic-philosophy) . Even after the intellectual revolutions of the [Renaissance](https://www.britannica.com/event/Renaissance) , the [Reformation](https://www.britannica.com/event/Reformation) , and the [Enlightenment](https://www.britannica.com/event/Enlightenment-European-history) , Aristotelian concepts remained embedded in Western [thinking](https://www.britannica.com/topic/thought) .

Aristotle’s intellectual range was vast, covering most of the sciences and many of the arts, including [biology](https://www.britannica.com/science/biology) , [botany](https://www.britannica.com/science/botany) , [chemistry](https://www.britannica.com/science/chemistry) , [ethics](https://www.britannica.com/topic/ethics-philosophy) , [history](https://www.britannica.com/topic/history) , [logic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/logic) , [metaphysics](https://www.britannica.com/topic/metaphysics) , [rhetoric](https://www.britannica.com/topic/rhetoric) , [philosophy of mind](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy-of-mind) , [philosophy of science](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy-of-science) , [physics](https://www.britannica.com/science/physics-science) , poetics, political theory, [psychology](https://www.britannica.com/science/psychology) , and [zoology](https://www.britannica.com/science/zoology) . He was the founder of [formal logic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/formal-logic) , devising for it a finished system that for centuries was regarded as the sum of the discipline; and he pioneered the study of zoology, both observational and theoretical, in which some of his work remained unsurpassed until the 19th century. But he is, of course, most outstanding as a philosopher. His writings in [ethics](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/ethics) and political theory as well as in [metaphysics](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/metaphysics) and the philosophy of science continue to be studied, and his work remains a powerful current in contemporary philosophical debate.

This article deals with Aristotle’s life and thought. For the later development of Aristotelian [philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy) , _see_ [Aristotelianism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Aristotelianism) . For treatment of Aristotelianism in the full [context](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/context) of Western philosophy, _see_ [philosophy, Western](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Western-philosophy) .

## Life

## The [Academy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Academy-ancient-academy-Athens-Greece)

Aristotle was born on the Chalcidic peninsula of Macedonia, in northern [Greece](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Greek-philosophy) . His father,Nicomachus, was the physician of [Amyntas III](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Amyntas-III) (reigned c. 393–c. 370 bce ), king of Macedonia and grandfather of [Alexander the Great](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Alexander-the-Great) (reigned 336–323 bce ). After his father’s death in 367, Aristotle migrated to [Athens](https://www.britannica.com/place/Athens) , where he joined the Academy of [Plato](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Plato) (c. 428–c. 348 bce ). He remained there for 20 years as Plato’s pupil and colleague.

Many of Plato’s later [dialogues](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/dialogues) date from these decades, and they may reflect Aristotle’s contributions to philosophical debate at the Academy. Some of Aristotle’s writings also belong to this period, though mostly they survive only in fragments. Like his master, Aristotle wrote initially in [dialogue](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/dialogue) form, and his early ideas show a strong [Platonic](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Platonic) influence. His dialogue _[Eudemus](https://www.britannica.com/topic/On-the-Soul-by-Aristotle)_ , for example, reflects the Platonic view of the [soul](https://www.britannica.com/topic/soul-religion-and-philosophy) as imprisoned in the body and as capable of a happier life only when the body has been left behind. According to Aristotle, the dead are more blessed and happier than the living, and to die is to return to one’s real home.

Britannica Quiz Ancient Greece

Another youthful work, the _Protrepticus_ (“Exhortation”), has been reconstructed by modern scholars from quotations in various works from late antiquity. Everyone must do philosophy, Aristotle claims, because even arguing against the practice of philosophy is itself a form of philosophizing. The best form of philosophy is the contemplation of the universe of nature; it is for this purpose that God made human beings and gave them a godlike intellect. All else—strength, beauty, power, and honour—is worthless.

It is possible that two of Aristotle’s surviving works on logic and disputation, the _[Topics](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Topics)_ and the _[Sophistical Refutations](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sophistical-Refutations)_ , belong to this early period. The former demonstrates how to construct arguments for a position one has already decided to adopt; the latter shows how to detect weaknesses in the arguments of others. Although neither work amounts to a systematic [treatise](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/treatise) on formal logic, Aristotle can justly say, at the end of the _Sophistical Refutations_ , that he has invented the [discipline](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/discipline) of logic—nothing at all existed when he started.

Explore Britannica Premium!

Smart, reliable knowledge for professionals, students, and curious minds everywhere.

[SUBSCRIBE](https://premium.britannica.com/premium-membership/?utm_source=premium&utm_medium=inline-cta&utm_campaign=smart-2026)

During Aristotle’s residence at the Academy, King [Philip II](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Philip-II-king-of-Macedonia) of Macedonia (reigned 359–336 bce ) waged war on a number of Greek [city-state](https://www.britannica.com/topic/city-state) s. The Athenians defended their independence only half-heartedly, and, after a series of humiliating [concessions](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/concessions) , they allowed Philip to become, by 338, master of the Greek world. It cannot have been an easy time to be a Macedonian resident in Athens.

Within the Academy, however, relations seem to have remained cordial. Aristotle always acknowledged a great debt to Plato; he took a large part of his philosophical agenda from Plato, and his teaching is more often a modification than a repudiation of Plato’s doctrines. Already, however, Aristotle was beginning to distance himself from Plato’s theory of Forms, or Ideas ( _eidos_ ; _see_ [form](https://www.britannica.com/topic/form-philosophy) ). (The word _Form_ , when used to refer to Forms as Plato [conceived](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/conceived) them, is often capitalized in the scholarly literature; when used to refer to forms as Aristotle conceived them, it is conventionally lowercased.) Plato had held that, in addition to particular things, there exists a suprasensible realm of Forms, which are immutable and everlasting. This realm, he maintained, makes particular things intelligible by accounting for their common natures: a thing is a horse, for example, by [virtue](https://www.britannica.com/topic/virtue-in-Christianity) of the fact that it shares in, or imitates, the Form of “Horse.” In a lost work, _On Ideas_ , Aristotle maintains that the arguments of Plato’s central dialogues establish only that there are, in addition to particulars, certain common objects of the sciences. In his surviving works as well, Aristotle often takes issue with the theory of Forms, sometimes politely and sometimes contemptuously. In his _[Metaphysics](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Metaphysica)_ he argues that the theory fails to solve the problems it was meant to address. It does not [confer](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/confer) intelligibility on particulars, because immutable and everlasting Forms cannot explain how particulars come into [existence](https://www.britannica.com/topic/existence) and undergo change. All the theory does, according to Aristotle, is introduce new entities equal in number to the entities to be explained—as if one could solve a problem by doubling it. ( _See below_ [Form](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Aristotle/Physics-and-metaphysics) .)

## Travels

When Plato died about 348, his nephew [Speusippus](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Speusippus) became head of the Academy, and Aristotle left Athens. He migrated to [Assus](https://www.britannica.com/place/Assus) , a city on the northwestern coast of Anatolia (in present-day Turkey), where [Hermias](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hermias) , a graduate of the Academy, was ruler. Aristotle became a close friend of Hermias and eventually married his ward Pythias. Aristotle helped Hermias to negotiate an alliance with Macedonia, which angered the Persian king, who had Hermias treacherously arrested and put to death about 341. Aristotle saluted Hermias’s [memory](https://www.britannica.com/science/memory-psychology) in “Ode to Virtue,” his only surviving poem.

While in Assus and during the subsequent few years when he lived in the city of Mytilene on the island of [Lesbos](https://www.britannica.com/place/Lesbos-island-Greece) , Aristotle carried out extensive scientific research, particularly in zoology and [marine biology](https://www.britannica.com/science/marine-biology) . This work was summarized in a book later known, misleadingly, as _[The History of Animals](https://www.britannica.com/topic/History-of-Animals)_ , to which Aristotle added two short [treatises](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/treatises) , _On the Parts of Animals_ and _On the Generation of Animals_ . Although Aristotle did not claim to have founded the [science](https://www.britannica.com/science/science) of zoology, his detailed observations of a wide variety of organisms were quite without precedent. He—or one of his research assistants—must have been gifted with remarkably [acute](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/acute) eyesight, since some of the features of insects that he accurately reports were not again observed until the invention of the microscope in the 17th century.

The scope of Aristotle’s scientific research is astonishing. Much of it is concerned with the classification of animals into genus and species; more than 500 species figure in his treatises, many of them described in detail. The [myriad](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/myriad) items of information about the anatomy, diet, habitat, modes of copulation, and reproductive systems of mammals, reptiles, fish, and insects are a melange of minute investigation and vestiges of superstition. In some cases his unlikely stories about rare species of fish were proved accurate many centuries later. In other places he states clearly and fairly a biological problem that took millennia to solve, such as the nature of embryonic development.

Despite an admixture of the fabulous, Aristotle’s biological works must be regarded as a stupendous achievement. His inquiries were conducted in a genuinely scientific spirit, and he was always ready to confess [ignorance](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/ignorance) where evidence was insufficient. Whenever there is a conflict between theory and observation, one must trust observation, he insisted, and theories are to be trusted only if their results conform with the observed phenomena.

In 343 or 342 Aristotle was summoned by Philip II to the Macedonian capital at Pella to act as tutor to Philip’s 13-year-old son, the future Alexander the Great. Little is known of the content of Aristotle’s instruction; although the _Rhetoric to Alexander_ was included in the Aristotelian corpus for centuries, it is now commonly regarded as a forgery. By 326 Alexander had made himself master of an empire that stretched from the Danube to the Indus and included Libya and Egypt. Ancient sources report that during his campaigns Alexander arranged for biological [specimens](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/specimens) to be sent to his tutor from all parts of Greece and [Asia Minor](https://www.britannica.com/place/Anatolia) .
